data.title: Alpha Tau Medical: Pancreatic, GBM, And Other Updates; Set Up For A Strong, Catalyst-Rich 2026
data.description:
Overview

It has been an eventful start to the year for Alpha Tau Medical (DRTS), which I first wrote about back in October as a unique technology/mechanism and a story set to inflect over 2026 and beyond. The stock is up ~70% since my initiation and ~35% YTD (after two rather aggressive days of retracement). Some of the events/headlines driving the move over the last couple of months include:

  • Visibility: Participation in investor conferences in December (Piper Sandler and Sidoti) and then the JPM Healthcare Conference last week. Alpha Tau was and still largely is an underknown name to biotech investors—these conference appearances have begun to help change that. Average daily volume has increased from sub-100k shares in the summer/fall to ~400k over the past month or so.

  • December 2nd: Clearance from the FDA to begin a trial in prostate cancer, marking its fifth active indication in the US (link).

  • December 9th: Treatment of its first GBM patient (link).

  • January 5th: The announcement that the FDA had agreed to the use of a more flexible, modular (essentially rolling) submission process for Alpha DaRT in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC), in which they expect to report pivotal data in 2H26. (Link). I think investors have come to appreciate that Alpha Tau receives unique treatment from the FDA, including its standing quarterly meeting with the agency to discuss and update on its various programs.

  • January 6th: Announced the final results from its first-in-human pancreatic cancer trial that was conducted in Montreal (link). As I discussed in my initiation, this study was a bit of a hodgepodge of different treatment lines, disease stages, etc., but demonstrates impressive activity in severely undertreated pancreatic cancer, especially considering the trial used only incomplete tumor coverage (tumors were only partially covered with DaRTs). Most of this data had already been reported, though there were some interesting new biomarker data.

The company also provided some interesting color at the JPM Conference last week, including:

  • cSCC regulatory pathway: Dosing is expected to be completed in 1Q26, with a pivotal data readout in 2H26. It expects to submit for approval in 2H26, and given its Breakthrough Device Designation (BDD), hopes to potentially receive priority review, making an early 2027 approval plausible.

  • Pancreatic update: Patient demand has been very strong—enrollment/dosing is expected to be completed in 1H26, with initial results in 2H26.

  • GBM update: The first GBM patient was dosed, which went “fantastically” (refers to the successful placement of Alpha DaRTs, not efficacy outcomes), achieving 95% tumor coverage, and the patient was able to go home within 24 hours.

  • Japanese PMA: The company still expects an approval decision from the Japanese PMDA regarding Alpha DaRT in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC).

I discuss more of management’s JPM commentary below, but the company appears to be on track or ahead of schedule for potential value-inflecting readouts and events in 2026.

There have also been some external/market factors that may be contributing to DRTS’ strong performance recently, namely:

  1. Rumors surrounding the acquisition of pancreatic-focused Revolution Medicines (RVMD).

  2. The success of alpha radiation biotech Aktis Oncology’s (AKTS) IPO.

In fact, the first question in the Q&A portion of Alpha Tau’s JPM presentation was about Revolution in pancreatic cancer and the implications for Alpha Tau in that indication. Management’s answer, which mirrored what I noted in my blurb/aside about Alpha Tau in my recent piece on Aktis, was that advancements in systemic therapies should only provide better combination therapy opportunities for Alpha DaRT, given its local application and very low toxicity profile (in stark contrast with most systemic therapies).

As I said in my 2025 Year-End Wrap-Up, Alpha Tau was one of my two or three favorite ideas coming into 2026, and while the stock move to start the year has eaten slightly into that return potential, the current ~$550 million market cap still appears quite attractive given Alpha DaRT’s unique positioning as the sole tumor-agnostic, localized application of alpha radiation in oncology, especially as we approach proof-of-concept US trial data in high-impact pancreatic and GBM indications in 2H26.

Pancreatic

For those following the Alpha Tau story, pancreatic and GBM have really become the two focal points, as both are severely undertreated.

At JPM, management reiterated that the ongoing PoC trial has been seeing “fantastic” interest from both patients and providers, primarily because of how few treatment options there are in the indication. Recall that patients are newly diagnosed with either locally advanced or metastatic disease and receive standard-of-care mFOLFIRINOX in combination with Alpha DaRT. According to ClinicalTrials, there are currently six actively recruiting sites in the US, including Emory University and NYU.

At JPM, the company stated that they expect to finish enrollment this quarter (essentially in line with prior guidance), with data expected in 2H26, after which the company intends to move quickly to apply to begin a pivotal trial.

Management did give some extra color regarding patients’ experience in the trial thus far, stating, “We have heard a lot of anecdotes about immediate pain relief following treatment,” which is significant as pancreatic cancer is known to involve intense localized pain in the abdomen.

As a reminder, the potential opportunity in pancreatic cancer is large (hence RevMed’s $23 billion valuation despite still being clinical-stage). The company estimates that 87% of the ~66k annual new patients have tumors that are unresectable.

The 30-patient readout in the 2H26 could be a significant catalyst, especially if it can compare favorably to something like Revolution Medicines’ daraxonrasib.

GBM

As stated above, the company enrolled its first GBM patient in early December at Ohio State University Medical Center.

CFO Raphi Levy stated in the JPM presentation that this patient’s procedure went “fantastically well,” achieving over 95% tumor coverage in a procedure that took ~1 hour, and that the patient was able to return home within 24 hours.

Taking a step back, it is pretty amazing that Alpha Tau was able to devise a way to apply Alpha DaRT precisely to patients’ brain tumors (the application technology is pictured below), an organ where, obviously, the surrounding tissue is extremely fragile and important to leave relatively undisturbed. That makes Alpha Tau’s achievement of 95% tumor coverage in a ~1-hour procedure and a sub-24-hour discharge extremely impressive.

The trial protocol dictates a cadence of one patient per month for the first three patients—the company says that the second patient is expected to be treated imminently (before the end of January)—after which enrollment will be allowed to increase and NYU will begin to enroll patients until they have enrolled all 10 patients. Management said during JPM that they expect to finish enrollment in 1H26, with data in 2H26, similar to pancreatic.

While things seem to be progressing well, the efficacy data could be a real potential catalyst for the stock. While speculation is cheap, if the company continues to be able to achieve ~95% tumor coverage, data in other cancer types suggests Alpha DaRT could be a potential breakthrough modality in GBM (in which it also happens to have Breakthrough Device Designation).

HNSCC

In my DRTS initiation piece, I discussed the impressive data generated from a small trial of Alpha DaRT in combination with Keytruda in recurrent or metastatic HNSCC. While there was no new data reported by the company at JPM, management’s discussion of the efficacy data during their presentation reminded me that a partnership with a larger pharma company (one that has a checkpoint inhibitor) would make a lot of sense in HNSCC and could be something that we see in 2026. Right now, Alpha Tau is still in a holding pattern with HNSCC, prioritizing other indications.

Additionally, Alpha Tau is still awaiting a potential PMDA approval in Japan for Alpha DaRT in HNSCC. Assuming this approval is achieved (it doesn’t feel completely certain given how much the timeline has been pushed back over the past several quarters), Alpha Tau plans to offer Alpha DaRT to some patients in Japan so as to satisfy Japanese regulators’ desire for post-approval patient data, though the company still plans to hold off on a full commercial launch until it is approved in the US.

2026 Catalysts and Regulatory Pathway

Alpha Tau’s playbook is clear: run small (n=10-30), quick, proof-of-concept trials in various indications (pancreatic, GBM, soon to be prostate) to demonstrate its impressive tumor-agnostic efficacy before applying to the FDA to begin a pivotal trial.

This strategy, along with its more mature cSCC program, is shaping up for an eventful 2026. The trials are enrolling as fast or faster than expected, and the FDA is clearly demonstrating an interest in allowing the company an expeditious path forward.

To reiterate, in 2026, we can expect:

  1. HNSCC approval in Japan (still) expected imminently.

  2. Final pivotal data in cSCC in 2H26, with a PMA submission and potential for priority review shortly thereafter. It seems likely that, even in the event of approval, the company would hold off on commercialization, based on prior comments about wanting to prioritize higher-impact underserved conditions (i.e., pancreatic, GBM).

  3. 30-patient pancreatic cancer data in 2H26 and potential advancement into a pivotal trial.

  4. 10-patient GBM data in 2H26 and potential advancement into a pivotal trial.

  5. Potential for a partnership of some sort with a large pharma, potentially in GBM, especially as the company gains more notoriety (and market cap) and reports more data in various indications.

(Based on management’s commentary, this graphic appears to be outdated with regard to GBM, which they state they expect to have initial data for in 2H26).

The pancreatic and GBM data in 2H26 represent major potential stock-moving catalysts.

Cash Position

The company should have adequate cash to comfortably get through all of these near- and medium-term catalysts, with $76 million of cash as of 9/30/25 and burning just $5-6 million per quarter (though this could increase a hair with the pancreatic and GBM trials in full swing, though both are small).

Revolution Medicines (RVMD)

Rumors have swirled over the past couple of weeks about both AbbVie and Merck potentially being in talks to acquire Revolution Medicines, which is developing a portfolio of RAS inhibitors for pancreatic cancer (and other cancers). This may have played a role in generating some attention for Alpha Tau, which concurrently put out a headline about its pancreatic trial data.

Interestingly, 90%+ of pancreatic cancer is driven by RAS mutations, meaning Revolution’s small molecule RAS inhibitor drugs are expected to treat almost all pancreatic cancer patients. To date, Revolution has reported data across different lines of treatment that suggest daraxonrasib, its lead candidate, will provide meaningful response and survival improvements over currently available treatments.

The first audience question at Alpha Tau’s JPM presentation asked about Revolution and what the approval of its RAS inhibitors could mean for Alpha Tau. CFO Raphi Levy’s response mirrored the thoughts I detailed in my Aktis Oncology piece, which is that Alpha DaRT’s tumor-agnostic mechanism and superb tolerability profile make it an ideal combination therapy for new systemic treatments, including RAS inhibitors like RevMed’s. If both Revolution and Alpha Tau are providing meaningful, yet still very far from universally curative, improvements versus SoC in pancreatic cancer, the combination of the two could have an additive or perhaps synergistic effect that moves us closer to actually providing lasting remission for more patients.

Revolution Medicines’ current market capitalization is $23 billion, which speaks to the valuation potential for oncology companies viewed as representing a breakthrough/paradigm shift.

Aktis IPO

Though I already touched on it in my recent piece on Aktis Oncology, I think the same dynamic applies to Aktis’ aspirations of utilizing systemic alpha-emitting radiopharmaceuticals to target specific tumor antigens.

While perhaps there is some extremely small chance that Aktis’ Nectin-4-targeting alpha radiopharmaceutical is so effective that it obviates the need for combination therapies, it seems much more likely that it, if successful, will move the efficacy frontier forward in various Nectin-4-expressing tumors (including GBM), but will still benefit from combination with a powerful, localized, low-side-effect treatment like Alpha DaRT (that has also shown to have immune-sensitizing effects).

Aktis’ current market cap is $1 billion, representing a ~75% premium to Alpha Tau. It is worth noting that while Aktis’ early imaging data suggest on-target activity, it has yet to report any actual efficacy data in humans.

Conclusion

Alpha Tau is a unique combination of

  1. A completely differentiated mechanism of action

  2. Situated within or adjacent to a “hot” space (radiopharmaceuticals, especially alpha particles)

  3. With a (very) large potential market opportunity (as a tumor-agnostic)

  4. And multiple potentially stock-moving readouts are expected over the next 6-12 months

2026 will likely mark Alpha Tau’s coming-out party in hindsight. At ~$550 million of market cap, I wouldn’t be surprised if Alpha Tau was up another 50-100% by the end of the year.

data.title: Top 5 Mining Stocks To Watch In 2026: No. 2 - Aris Mining
data.description:
The year 2026 is here, and it is time for the traditional "Top 5 Mining Stocks to Watch" list. 2025 was a huge success, as the record-breaking metals prices pushed the featured companies to triple-digit returns. As of December 29, Aris Mining (ARMN) was up 360%, followed by Taseko Mines (TGB) (186%), and Eldorado Gold (EGO) (141%). In the case of Calibre Mining, the calculation is a little more complex, as in June, the company was acquired by Equinox Gold (EQX). However, taking into account the 2024 closing price of Calibre, and the current value of 0.31 Equinox shares, Calibre is technically nearly 102% up. The least successful pick was Franco-Nevada (FNV), although a 78% gain can hardly be taken as a failure. On average, the 2025 Top 5 list gained 191.4%, the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) (169%), the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) (154%), the SPDR S&P Metals and Mining ETF (XME) (86%), and also the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) (17%).

It is not realistic to expect similar gains also in 2026, as the gold, silver, and copper prices will most probably ease the pace of growth (and possibly even give up a portion of the 2025 gains), however, numerous mining companies expect some major catalysts and have a good probability of outperforming their peers. Just like every year, I know that there are definitely some other companies with high potential that could have been considered for inclusion in the Top 5. If you are missing such a company on the list, please feel free to mention it in the comments section.

The fifth place on the 2026 list was captured by NexGold Mining (NXGCF), the fourth place was captured by U.S. Gold Corp. (USAU), and the third place was captured by Taseko Mines. Position No. 2 belongs to Aris Mining. Aris was also No. 2 on the 2025 list, and it recorded great returns. However, it entered the year 2025 significantly undervalued, and given the steep gold price growth, and the progress achieved at its development projects, Aris is undervalued also today. It is hard to expect nearly 400% gains also in 2026; however, triple-digit gains should be achievable quite easily if everything goes well.

The Current State of Aris Mining

Aris is operating two mines in Colombia. At the Segovia mine, a second mill was commissioned in June 2025. Segovia is expected to produce around 265,000-300,000 toz gold in 2026, as the ramp-up to the projected full capacity of 300,000 to gold per year continues. In 2025, the mine produced 231,177 toz gold. The current reserves of 1.5 million toz gold should suffice for the next 5 years of operations; however, the measured, indicated, and inferred resources contain nearly 6.5 million toz gold, which means that only less than 1/4 of the resources have been converted into reserves so far. Moreover, Aris keeps on drilling and discovering more gold at Segovia, so the actual mine life will end up significantly longer than the currently projected 5 years.

The Marmato mine produced only 28,741 toz gold in 2025; however, the small-scale operation is about to be expanded significantly. The Marmato bulk mining zone operation is under construction, which should be completed during the summer. The targeted full capacity of around 200,000 toz gold per year should be reached by the end of 2027. However, the first fruits of the expansion should be seen in H2 2026, and Marmato is targeted to produce 35,000-50,000 toz gold this year. The current reserves of 3.18 million toz gold should suffice for approximately 15 years. However, the reserves represent only around 36% of the currently known resources. It means that, similar to Segovia, the Marmato mine life will almost surely be expanded significantly.

Besides the two operating mines, there are also two advanced-stage projects. The more advanced one, Toroparu, is located in Guayana. Its measured and indicated resources contain 5.31 million toz gold, and inferred resources contain a further 1.18 million toz gold. Toroparu is fully permitted, and its previous owner has even initiated the early construction activities. However, after acquiring the project, Aris decided to stop the works and optimize the project. The result is a PEA that envisions an average annual production of 235,000 toz gold at an AISC of $1,289-toz, over a 21-year mine life. The initial CAPEX was projected at $830 million. Toroparu has an after-tax NPV (5%) of $1.8 billion at the base-case gold price of $3,000/toz. At a gold price of $4,000/toz, it is $3.24 billion. A PFS for Toroparu should be completed by H2 2026, and the construction decision should follow shortly after.

And there is also the Soto Norte project in Colombia. Aris consolidated its ownership to 100% only recently. The project contains reserves of 4.57 million toz gold, which represents only around 42% of the currently known resources. According to the PFS released in September, the mine should be producing around 203,000 toz gold per year at an AISC of only $534/toz, over a 22-year mine life. The AISC is so low due to meaningful copper by-product credits. Moreover, over the first 10 years, the production should average 263,000 toz gold per year. The initial CAPEX was projected at $625 million, which is a very reasonable price to develop a mining operation of this size. Therefore, the economics of the project are very attractive. The after-tax NPV (5%) is estimated at $3.27 billion, at a gold price of $3,000/toz. However, it is as much as $4.75 billion at a gold price of $4,000/toz.

It is also important to mention Aris Mining's highly experienced management team. Worth mentioning is especially the CEO of the company, Neil Woodyer. He is the former founder and CEO of Endeavour Mining and Leagold Mining. And as Aris Mining's Chair of the Board of Directors, Ian Telfer, the former CEO of Goldcorp, and the former Chairman of the World Gold Council, serves. He is also a member of the Canadian Business Hall of Fame, as well as the Canadian Mining Hall of Fame.

The 2026 Catalysts

Several catalysts are expected in 2026. First of all, the Segovia mine ramp-up continues, and production of 265,000-300,000 toz gold is expected in 2026. Marmato bulk zone should start ramping up in H2 2026. The full capacity of 200,000 toz gold per year should be reached by 2027. In 2026, 35,000-50,000 toz gold is expected from Marmato. It means that if everything goes well, Aris Mining should produce 300,000-350,000 toz gold in 2026.

In Q3 2025, Aris produced 73,236 toz gold and generated an operating cash flow of $105.6 million, at an average realized gold price of $3,494/toz. It means that at a gold price of $3,500/toz, production of 325,000 toz gold should lead to an operating cash flow of $470 million. At a gold price of $4,000/toz, it should be closer to $600 million (of course, these calculations are simplified, based on an extrapolation of the Q3 2025 financial results). The median price-to-operating cash flow ratio in the mining industry is 15.96 right now. Using a more conservative value of 10 leads to a market capitalization of $6 billion. This is 50% more than Aris Mining's current market capitalization of $4 billion. Moreover, this calculation is based on a gold price of $4,000/toz, which looks relatively conservative, as the current market price is around $4,800/toz. It means that Segovia and Marmato alone should provide significant upside at the current gold prices. Any news confirming that the Segovia and Marmato operations are on track to reach the guidance should serve as positive catalysts.

Moreover, in H2 2026, the PFS for the Toroparu project should be released. It is possible to expect some improvements to the economics of the project. The company plans to move to the construction phase after the PFS is completed. Given that Toroparu is fully permitted, this may mean that the construction will start in early 2027. However, more clarity regarding the schedule should be known after the PFS is released. A schedule envisaging relatively rapid construction and ramp-up can serve as another catalyst.

And another catalyst, expected sometime in H1 2026, is the application for an environmental license for Soto Norte.

Conclusion

Of course, there are also risks. First of all, it is the gold price. It is at record highs, in the $4,800/toz area. Only a year ago, such a high price was hard to imagine. A correction will come sooner or later, and it will have a negative short-term impact on the share price of Aris Mining. However, if it stabilizes at a reasonable level, say in the $3,500-4,000 area, Aris will still have a triple-digit upside potential compared to its current share price.

In the case of Aris, the jurisdiction risk must also be taken into account. Segovia, Marmato, and Soto Norte are located in Colombia, which is a country well known for security issues. Toroparu is located in Guyana, and it will provide some geographical diversification, but not much. A sizeable operation in a safer country, like the USA, Canada, or Australia, would be a nice addition.

There is also the permitting risk related to Soto Norte and, obviously, also operational risks related to the Marmato bulk zone ramp-up, Toroparu construction and ramp-up, and later also Soto Norte construction and ramp-up. What is positive, Aris is in a very good financial condition, with a cash of $390 million as of the end of 2025, and debt of $518 million as of the end of Q3. At the current gold prices, there is a good chance to finance Toroparu and Soto Norte constructions from cash flows and debt, avoiding share dilution completely, or limiting it to a very reasonable level.

However, the risks are outweighed by the significant upside potential. As calculated above, it is possible to expect an operating cash flow of around $600 million in 2026, at a gold price of $4,000/toz. This should warrant at least a $6 billion market capitalization, or a share price of around $30, which is 50% above its current level. However, this calculation doesn't reflect the fact that the Marmato bulk zone should push Aris Mining's production level to 500,000 toz gold per year by the end of 2027. And it doesn't attribute any value to Toroparu and Soto Norte, which have a combined after-tax NPV (5%) of $8 billion at a gold price of $4,000/toz.

In other words, if everything goes according to plan, Aris Mining's share price may double quite easily before the end of 2026, even if the gold price calms down and consolidates around the $4,000/toz level or slightly lower.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why PSI Is Fighting The Wrong Risk
data.description:
The Invesco Semiconductors ETF (PSI) promises a lot of risk management, and early momentum and fundamental improvement detection potential, but falls short of the current market regime where leadership is acting both as a growth lever and as a defense. The end result is that PSI manages to offer some upside compared to an equal-weighted semis portfolio, but does not provide either the growth or defense of a leadership-concentrated portfolio. The concentration risks it handles and the smart multi-approach methodology it provides are not likely to show up benefits unless the semis markets broaden - which seems unlikely as of now.

Methodology and Portfolio Repercussion

The PSI methodology (tracking the Dynamic Semiconductor Intellidex Index) is very well balanced and relies on several qualitative indicators for portfolio selection. However, the portfolio size is still around 30, which makes weighting a far more important variable determining performance outcomes. The selection scoring model looks at price momentum, earnings momentum, quality, management action, and value.

This quantitative framework means PSI is structurally designed to benefit most when earnings momentum and fundamental improvements broaden across the semiconductor universe, rather than remaining concentrated in a handful of dominant names. In a traditional cyclical upturn, such a model would typically allow PSI to rotate into improving mid- and smaller-cap stocks earlier than market-cap-weighted portfolios.

The 30 stocks that are selected using this methodology are eventually divided into two groups. One group consists of the top 8 stocks in terms of market cap, and 40% of the portfolio is equally allocated among them (5% weight each on average). The remaining 22 stocks account for the remaining 60% and are also equally weighted at rebalance (~2.73% per stock on average).

Overall, the sophistication and promise in the methodology do not influence the overall portfolio construction. It helps selection hygiene - but even here the group of ~30 holdings is already close to what most semis portfolios anyway end up with given business classification and revenue restrictions.

The only benefit that clearly stands out in PSI's methodology, irrespective of how the semis market is structured across regimes, is that it dampens the concentration risks to a large extent. However, it does respect leadership by allocating almost double the weight to leaders than smaller caps. This partial market cap approach with a dampening effect is very much visible in its top 10 holdings. The top 10 holdings here contribute only ~46% to the portfolio by weight. For context, SMH, a market cap weighted ETF, has around 74% weight in the top 10. Critically, Nvidia occupies around 18% of the portfolio in SMH and is subdued in PSI. It is still in the top 10 and could be at the top too, depending on the drifts between rebalances. However, by definition, it starts at 5% every rebalance (as long as it is in the top 8 by market weight) and depends on intra-quarter price movements to determine its intermediate rank and weight.

Another important thing to note about PSI is that it remains US-centric, which means it excludes two big names that are heavyweights in SMH-like global portfolios - TSMC and ASML. Otherwise, PSI ticks most of the boxes in terms of diversification. It has a fair share of equipment, materials, and device companies, and allocations here are further helped by the quasi-equal weighted approach.

Combining the methodology and portfolio view, it can be safely concluded that momentum-led leaders dominate the portfolio (but portfolio leadership is eventually subdued due to equal weighting). The quarterly rebalancing with a model that favors momentum implies stocks that are seeing high momentum can walk into the portfolio faster than if they were waiting for market caps to reflect their quality. In short, PSI can chase winners early compared to how a backward looking market cap based approach would enable it. The second thing that differentiates PSI is a greater presence of smaller-cap names. These smaller-cap stocks are quality screened too. Thirdly, several names with lesser exposure to the secular AI tailwinds, like Intel, are allocated high weights because of their size and may end up introducing cyclical risks instead of risk-adjusted growth.

Learnings from Performance

For performance context, I have benchmarked PSI's total returns against SMH (as representative of concentrated market cap based portfolios) and XSD (equal weighted approach). PSI itself is quasi- equal weighted with only 8 stocks assigned higher weights, but that too not by a distance - something we see in SMH-like portfolios. However, its leadership and quality tilt show up in bull markets for semis, where it outperforms XSD.

The underperformance against SMH is material. And that too boils down to the leadership concentration that has worked out well for market cap based portfolios. For the concentration risks in SMH though, we do not see any commensurate drawdown benefit in PSI in unconducive markets (like in 2021-23) for semis.

This is an important observation when extrapolating expectations for 2026. Even in periods where the semiconductor cycle stabilizes or improves, leadership-driven earnings strength has continued to dominate returns, limiting the upside capture of strategies that dampen concentration without delivering downside protection. This underperformance in rallies and comparable performance at best, in mid-cycle or early-cycle regimes, reveals an important aspect of how the semis markets are structured today, as we will discuss in the next section.

Outlook and Recommendation

The critical aspect that emerges from the performance data is that market leadership in semis acts both as the growth lever as well as defensives. That is to say, Nvidia is likely to fall lower in market-wide stress regimes and is best placed to deliver growth too. And if that is indeed the case, do concentration risks (something PSI handles well) matter at all?

Theoretically, I see merit in concentration today. The few larger players in semis today deliver earnings-led growth due to pricing power and monopoly-like demand. And the same stocks become better defenses too because they have stronger balance sheets, remain mission critical in the AI infra space, and benefit from secular demand today (from AI tailwinds).

My outlook for 2026 is that this structural demand backdrop is unlikely to materially change. While cyclical factors such as inventory normalization or selective capex recovery may support earnings momentum across a broader set of semiconductor companies, a narrow group of market leaders will still dominate growth. In such an environment, PSI’s quantitative emphasis on earnings momentum and fundamentals is more likely to capture marginal improvements, rather than structural outperformance.

This regime is not likely to change going ahead as well. So, what we have seen in the past in terms of leadership rotation, equipment demand lagging late cycle, and cyclical memory demand trends is effectively being reset. That decreases the odds of equal-weight or similarly unconcentrated portfolios benefiting either in late-cycle corrections, or in broad bullish conditions - something that could have helped PSI's approach in the past.

Unless we see a regime when leadership mean reverts, cycles become important again, and smaller caps benefit in broader rallies, PSI is ending up managing risks that are more theoretical than expected. PSI mitigates any shock to Nvidia earnings (or idiosyncratic stock-specific risks of market leaders), and may be well poised in an environment where hyperscaler demand weakens. But that is not where I see the semis market going.

Therefore, PSI becomes a hold in my opinion. I would not recommend adding fresh capital here because the semis leadership is unlikely to broaden, looking at the current moat-like positions the leaders are in. Even if there is consolidation like we are seeing over the past few months, or mild corrections, I see money rotating away from smaller caps into more certain large-cap leadership - i.e., Nvidia, Broadcom, AMD, ASML and the like (something SMH and market-cap weighted portfolios are loaded up with).

data.title: Osterweis Capital Management Q1 2026 Equity Outlook
data.description:
The AI Effect: Haves & Have-Nots

Looking back at the financial headlines of 2025, you would be excused if you thought the health of the economy was broad and deep: the S&P 500 Index saw a total return of 17.9%, U.S. real GDP accelerated to 4.3% annual growth in the third quarter of the year, and unemployment stayed relatively tame at 4.4% as of December 2025.

However, 2025 also saw the highest rate of U.S. corporate bankruptcies in 15 years, eclipsed only by the post-Great Financial Crisis highs last seen in 2010. So-called “mega bankruptcies,” or bankruptcy filings by companies with over $1 billion in assets, also surged. And the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index saw a healthy, but much lower, total return of 11.4% vs. its market cap-weighted equivalent (the standard S&P 500).

In this quarter’s outlook, we examine why the economy looks so healthy on the one hand and more precarious on the other, and we elaborate on how we are positioning client portfolios to navigate this evident divergence.

A Brief Review of Recent Market History

While it is natural to look back at the last three years - which have been dominated by the AI narrative ever since ChatGPT’s public release on November 30, 2022 - and marvel at how strong market returns have been since then, there has in fact been substantial volatility and uncertainty under the hood.

Recall that the spring of 2023 saw a rash of regional bank failures driven by rapid interest rate hikes by the Federal Reserve, leading to concerns of a broader banking crisis that never materialized, given large banks’ well-capitalized balance sheets.

Then 2024 saw persistent uncertainty leading up to the presidential election, punctuated by an unexpected interest rate increase by the Bank of Japan that forced a rapid unwind of the Yen carry trade and drove a sharp tech selloff before a strong recovery in the fourth quarter.

And 2025 started with massive public sector layoffs, a litany of regulatory and other government changes, and the unleashing of an unprecedented tariff regime that has since been largely walked back, in addition to the so-called “DeepSeek moment” that called into question the wisdom of rampant AI spending.

The net result has been a three-year bull market, albeit one driven by narrow sectors of the economy. In fact, the S&P 500 Index has generated an eye-popping 23% annualized return over the last three years, while the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index has generated a more prosaic, but still impressive, 12.8% annualized return.

To frame it another way, the market multiples of the S&P 500 Index and its sister Equal Weight Index were tightly bound for the decade leading up to 2022 but have widely diverged since then.

We think this divergence in returns and market multiples speaks to the underlying issues facing the economy: all things AI are growing rapidly, while tariffs have clobbered certain sectors of the U.S. economy by elongating the inflation cycle, and higher interest rates are biting companies and consumers that previously relied on cheap debt.

The AI Haves

According to The Wall Street Journal, as much as half of real GDP growth in the first six months of 2025 was driven by business investment in AI. The divergence between the AI economy and the rest of the economy is well captured by a BofA analysis that estimates private nonresidential fixed investment (i.e., investments in physical assets, including plants, machinery, construction, etc.) has roughly tripled for data centers since the end of 2022, while all other nonresidential fixed investment has been roughly flat over that same timeframe.

While the AI investment boom deserves careful scrutiny given the scale of investment by a handful of companies - Goldman Sachs estimates hyperscalers invested nearly $400 billion in CapEx in 2025, equivalent to roughly 1% of U.S. GDP - our research indicates that large companies are seeing tangible returns from implementing AI for everything from better targeting of online advertising, to coding efficiencies, to improved customer service, to predictive manufacturing, to better routing and logistics.

As a result, we believe the AI boom is sustainable and the investment appears to be justified thus far. However, we are ever vigilant in monitoring companies’ investment plans and the returns (or lack thereof) on AI spend.

Maybe most importantly, the so-called Magnificent 7 companies, many of which are deeply involved in AI spend, generated roughly 27% EPS growth in 2025, according to Bloomberg, reflecting significant financial benefits from the AI investment cycle.

The Non-AI Have Nots

As it relates to inflation and tariffs, the industrial and consumer discretionary sectors appear to have been hurt the most. In fact, these two sectors by far account for the largest number of U.S. corporate bankruptcies. We attribute this concentration to the fact that many industrial and consumer discretionary businesses import products from abroad.

Already facing persistent inflation from the post-Covid supply chain crisis, the rise in average tariffs from roughly 2% entering 2025 to roughly 11% as of September 2025 has meant that companies reliant on imports have faced massive further cost increases for certain inputs.

If a company lacks pricing power and/or is not nimble enough to shift production, the heavier burden can crush already strained profitability almost overnight. Add in the fact that interest rates moved significantly higher over the course of 2022 and have largely stayed elevated despite recent rate cuts, and companies with strained balance sheets cannot survive, or are forced to just limp along.

Unfortunately, many average consumers must deal with these same pressures. While incomes have increased in recent years, inflation has persisted, made only worse by the tariff regime, saddling consumers with ever higher prices on everything from apparel, to automobiles, to food. Add in rising interest rates on credit card debt, auto loans, and home mortgages, and consumers are also facing a litany of headwinds.

Reflecting these realities, the ISM Manufacturing PMI has been below 50, indicating a contracting U.S. manufacturing base, for ten straight months since March 2025. In fact, the U.S. manufacturing base has been in contraction since November 2022, with the exception of just two months in early 2025, before the tariff regime came into effect. Furthermore, consumer confidence is hovering at 45-year lows.

We would note at least three mitigating factors. First, the Federal Reserve began cutting rates in 2025, easing the interest cost burden on both companies and consumers. Second, both business and consumer leverage are at healthy levels, dramatically reducing the risk of a debt-fueled crisis in our view. Third, high-income spenders are making up for softness among low-income consumers, as the top 10% of earners now account for roughly half of all consumer spending.

Consistent with the narrative above, the S&P 500 Index, excluding the Magnificent 7, grew EPS at roughly 12% in 2025, according to Bloomberg. While growing at less than half the rate of the Magnificent 7, the non-AI economy is still expanding, and we believe the largest companies in the economy (those in the S&P 500) are much better positioned than most others given competitive advantages they enjoy like economies of scale.

In fact, The Wall Street Journal recently ran a story detailing how “over the past six months, private firms with fewer than 50 workers have steadily shed jobs… cutting 120,000 [jobs] in November alone.”

Meanwhile, midsize and especially large firms have in fact added jobs over the same timeframe. The article explains this widening gap by pointing out that “small-business profits are faltering” while larger businesses are better equipped to deal with the current economic headwinds and grow.

How We Are Positioning Portfolios

As noted above, we view the economy as increasingly divided between AI and non-AI companies. We believe there are merits to investing in businesses in both camps, especially when considering valuation discrepancies between the two.

We continue to invest in companies exposed to AI, although our investments in this area are in businesses that we believe will continue to thrive even if there are bumps in the road in the AI investment cycle.

At the same time, we continue to invest aggressively in non-AI players operating in other sectors like financials, industrials, consumer, and health care, but with a strong preference for sectors benefitting from secular tailwinds. This helps ensure persistent growth in most environments.

Regardless, we believe every position held in client portfolios fits our quality growth framework, underpinned by strong competitive advantages reflected in growing free cash flow, a reinvestment opportunity to drive future growth, and strong governance.

When purchased at reasonable valuations, we believe these companies will generate attractive investment returns, regardless of what theme is driving markets at any one moment.

Concluding Thoughts

While headlines reflect a strong and growing economy, there are many crosscurrents that must be examined. AI investment is booming, and for good reason in our view, but is not without risk.

Meanwhile, the non-AI economy in general is exhibiting slower growth with a broad range of outcomes, from small, poorly managed and undercapitalized businesses facing many headwinds to well-run, excellent companies posting solid growth.

We believe that owning a select basket of well-researched, quality growth businesses at attractive valuations spanning the AI and non-AI economy should reward patient, long-term-oriented investors.

We thank you for your continued confidence in our management, and we wish you a prosperous and happy new year.

data.title: How Putwrite ETFs Seek Option-Premium Income: A Deep Dive With Overlay Shares
data.description:
By Eric McArdle

If you work with income-focused investors, you’ve probably heard some version of this question: “Is there any way to generate more income without taking on risks I don’t understand?”

It’s not an easy problem. Bond markets have gone through large interest rate shifts, equities can be volatile, and many investors still remember how “hidden risks” surfaced in past drawdowns.

This has led some advisors and institutions to explore options-based strategies, including putwrite ETFs, as one possible component of an income-oriented portfolio. While this blog discusses put-writing concepts broadly, Overlay Shares implements the strategy through put spreads, pairing each short put with a lower-strike long put to establish a defined-risk options overlay.

First: What is a putwrite ETF?

At a high level, a putwrite ETF is an exchange-traded fund that:

  1. Sells (“writes”) put options on a market index (often the S&P 500 Index), and

  2. Holds collateral - such as cash, short-term fixed income, or an underlying ETF - to support the obligations of those options.

When the ETF sells a put option, it:

  • Receives an upfront option premium, and

  • Takes on the obligation to absorb some downside if the index falls below the strike price of the option.

If the index finishes above the strike price at expiration, the put option may expire worthless, and the strategy keeps the premium. If the index falls below the strike price, the put seller may incur losses that can partially or fully offset, or exceed, that premium.

In simple terms, the strategy is agreeing to take on some downside risk in exchange for option premium income, within a rules-based framework.

Why option premiums may exist in the market

One factor that has influenced option pricing over time is the historical tendency for implied volatility to exceed the volatility the market ultimately realizes. This relationship - often referred to as the volatility risk premium - has been observed across multiple market periods, although it can vary meaningfully and may not persist in the future.

It does not eliminate risk, and it should not be viewed as a guarantee of income or positive returns. However, understanding this market dynamic can help explain why option sellers may at times receive premiums for taking on equity market risk.

Why sell puts instead of calls?

When advisors hear “options-based income,” many immediately think of covered-call strategies. If the goal is to harvest option premium, though, there are important differences between selling calls and selling puts. The historical data behind the chart below helps illustrate this.

We start by comparing two option selling indexes: the Cboe S&P 500 BuyWrite Index (BXM, black) and the Cboe S&P 500 PutWrite T-W Index (PWT, blue). BXM consists of a portfolio of S&P 500 Index securities with a monthly at-the-money (ATM) call option sold against the portfolio.

PWT owns U.S. Treasury Bills and sells a monthly ATM put option on the S&P 500 Index. To reiterate: PWT does not include S&P 500 portfolio exposure. Over this period, investors in a systematic treasury bill + putwrite strategy (PWT) have outperformed the covered-call overlay strategy (BXM) without having any direct equity beta exposure. The sample below shows data from 7/7/2014 – 12/31/2025.

Source: Ycharts | Results vary across market cycles and index performance is not indicative of any investable strategy. You cannot invest directly in the index

The pattern is intuitive. A covered-call strategy accepts call premium but gives up a portion of the market’s strongest upside moves, while still participating in most drawdowns. In long or powerful equity rallies, that forgone upside can more than offset the income.

By contrast, a putwrite strategy is not limited in its upside potential. Selling puts can allow investors to keep the full premium in flat or rising markets and use that premium to help absorb part of the damage in more moderate pullbacks.

Only when markets move meaningfully below the strike does the options sleeve begin to look and feel more like equity risk. Additionally, option premiums may not be sufficient to offset losses in declining markets.

For income-focused investors, the practical takeaway is that not all option-selling frameworks are created equal. For some investors, selling puts may offer a differentiated way to seek option-premium income across a full market cycle.

This is one of the reasons the Overlay Shares lineup is built around a disciplined, out-of-the-money put-writing framework, while still acknowledging that each approach will have periods where it is more or less favorable.

But not all putwrite strategies look alike. Two design choices can significantly affect how they behave:

  1. What collateral backs the options?

    Two broad approaches are common:

  • Cash-secured putwriting

    The fund sells put options and holds enough cash and/or short-term instruments to cover its obligations if the market declines sharply. This can help manage counterparty obligations, but it does not eliminate the risk of loss. If the market falls, the value of the options position can decline substantially.

  • Overlay on an existing portfolio

    Some strategies, including Overlay Shares, hold a core ETF exposure - such as a large-cap equity ETF or a core bond ETF - and then apply a put option overlay on top. In that case, investors are exposed both to:

    • The underlying asset class (equities or bonds), and

    • The performance of the options overlay.

The choice of collateral affects the strategy’s return pattern, risk exposures, and potential role in a portfolio.

  1. Single puts vs. put spreads

A second key choice is whether the strategy uses:

  • Single (“naked”) short puts

    The strategy sells a put option, collects premium, and remains exposed to additional losses as the index declines, potentially down to zero in an extreme scenario.

  • Put spreads

    A put spread is created by:

    • Selling a put at one strike price, and

    • Buying another put at a lower strike price.

The purchased (long) put is designed to create a floor on losses from the options position over each option cycle. While this does not protect the overall portfolio from losses in the underlying assets, it helps define the maximum loss attributable to the options sleeve.

Overlay Shares uses put spreads, rather than single short puts, within its overlay design.

How Overlay Shares uses a put spread overlay

Liquid Strategies manages the Overlay Shares family of ETFs, which focuses on combining core equity and fixed income ETF exposures with a put spread overlay using options on the S&P 500 Index.

Conceptually, the process looks like this:

  1. Core ETF exposure

    Each fund starts with a core allocation - such as:

    • An S&P 500 ETF (U.S. large-cap equities)

    • A small-cap equity ETF

    • A foreign equity ETF

    • A core bond ETF

    • A short-term bond ETF

    • A municipal bond ETF

  2. Put spread overlay on each ETF’s respective underlying index

    On top of that core, the strategy systematically:

      • Sells laddered, out-of-the-money (OTM) index put options, and

      • Purchases lower-strike index put options, forming put spreads

  3. Defined options risk framework

    Because each short put is paired with a lower-strike long put, the options sleeve has a defined maximum loss over each option cycle. However, the underlying holding still carries downside market risk. This does not eliminate equity or bond market risk, but it provides a structural framework for managing tail risk within the overlay itself.

  4. Application across multiple asset classes

The Overlay Shares ETF lineup applies this same overlay concept across:

    • U.S. large-cap equities

    • U.S. small-cap equities

    • Foreign equities

    • Core bonds

    • Short-term bonds

    • Municipal bonds

For some investors, the appeal is the combination of familiar core exposures with a systematic options overlay that is designed with risk controls on the derivatives sleeve.

Where might putwrite overlays fit in a portfolio?

Any decision to use a putwrite or overlay approach should be grounded in an investor’s objectives, risk tolerance, constraints, and time horizon. Conceptually, some investors and advisors evaluate strategies like Overlay Shares in a few broad contexts:

  1. Adjusting core equity allocations

    An investor who already holds a broad equity ETF might, together with their adviser:

  • Maintain a core allocation to a traditional equity ETF, and

  • Allocate a portion to an ETF that combines equity or bond exposure with a put spread overlay.

The idea is to stay connected to major markets while introducing a rules-based options component that may change the pattern of returns and distributions.

  1. Building a diversified “income sleeve”

    Some income-focused portfolios blend multiple sources of potential cash flow, such as:

  • Traditional fixed income

  • Dividend-oriented equities

  • Options-based strategies, including putwrite and overlay approaches

Because Overlay Shares applies a consistent overlay concept across multiple underlying asset classes (equities and fixed income), some investors view the family as one potential tool to diversify how income is generated—across coupons, dividends, and option premiums—recognizing that this does not eliminate risk but offers a potentially diversifying risk premium.

  1. Incorporating a defined options risk framework

    Investors who are comfortable with derivatives but prefer clearly defined parameters may gravitate toward structures, like put spreads, that establish a maximum loss for the options sleeve over each option cycle. This does not cap losses in the overall fund, but it offers a more transparent framework for understanding the risk contribution of the overlay itself.

In all of these cases, position sizing, monitoring, and rebalancing are critical and typically require ongoing review.

Risk Factors

Options strategies involve risk, including the potential for significant losses. Putwrite and put spread strategies may lose more than the premiums received, particularly in sharply declining markets. Premium income is not guaranteed and may vary with market conditions. Past performance of indices or strategies is not indicative of future results.

Selling (writing) and buying options are speculative activities and entail greater than ordinary investment risks. The Fund’s use of put options can lead to losses because of adverse movements in the price or value of the underlying asset, which may be magnified by certain features of the options. When selling a put option, the Fund will receive a premium; however, this premium may not be enough to offset a loss incurred by the Fund if the price of the underlying asset is below the strike price by an amount equal to or greater than the premium. Purchased put options may expire worthless and the Fund would lose the premium it paid for the option. The Fund may lose significantly more than the premiums it receives in highly volatile market conditions.

The Fund will invest in short term put options which are financial derivatives that give buyers the right, but not the obligation, to sell (put) an underlying asset at an agreed-upon price and date. The Fund’s use of options may reduce the Fund’s ability to profit from increases in the value of the underlying asset. The Fund could experience a loss or increased volatility if its derivatives do not perform as anticipated or are not correlated with the performance of their underlying asset or if the Fund is unable to purchase or liquidate a position.
